content.text: FastAPI is the dominant Python framework for building HTTP APIs that back agentic systems. Its combination of Python type hints, Pydantic data validation, and native async support makes it the natural home for agent endpoints, tool servers, and orchestration APIs where correctness, speed, and developer ergonomics all matter simultaneously.

FastAPI is an ASGI web framework that reads standard Python type annotations to drive request validation, response serialization, and interactive API documentation simultaneously. You declare what a route expects as ordinary Python function parameters; FastAPI and Pydantic do the rest. A path parameter typed as
is automatically parsed and coerced; a request body typed as a Pydantic
is automatically validated against a JSON schema before your handler runs. Routes are defined with decorators such as
, but handlers can be either sync functions or
coroutines. Underneath, FastAPI is a thin layer on top of Starlette (ASGI toolkit) and Pydantic (validation). It ships with automatic OpenAPI and JSON Schema generation, interactive Swagger UI at

Agentic systems make many outbound calls — to LLM APIs, vector stores, tool servers, memory stores — and receive results asynchronously. A synchronous server would waste a thread for each in-flight call, capping throughput at worker count. FastAPI's async-first design lets a single process handle hundreds of concurrent requests while those external calls are awaited. Beyond concurrency, agents exchange structured data: tool inputs, chain outputs, structured model responses. FastAPI's Pydantic integration means every piece of that data is validated at the boundary, catching schema mismatches early rather than propagating corrupt state through a multi-step pipeline.
— Pydantic enforces types on every request body, path parameter, and query string; invalid input returns a 422 with a detailed error message automatically
— async def handlers and httpx / aiohttp clients scale concurrently without threading overhead
— OpenAPI spec and Swagger UI come for free; client libraries can be generated from the spec without extra work
— the built-in Depends() system cleanly manages database sessions, auth tokens, and shared services without globals
— Python type hints serve as contracts; mypy and pyright catch interface mismatches before runtime
— Pydantic v2's Rust core makes schema checks orders of magnitude faster than pure-Python validators
— built-in BackgroundTasks fires after-response work without a separate Celery cluster for lightweight use cases
— mixing sync blocking calls inside async handlers starves the event loop; all I/O must be async-native or offloaded to a thread pool
— FastAPI 0.100+ uses Pydantic v2, which is faster but has breaking API changes versus v1; older tutorials may show deprecated patterns
— SQLAlchemy, SQLModel, or Tortoise ORM must be wired in manually, adding setup compared to Django's batteries-included ORM
— deeply nested Depends() chains can become hard to trace; explicit dependency trees require deliberate organization as the app grows
— the Depends() pattern is powerful but unfamiliar to developers coming from Flask or Django; scoping (request vs. global) must be understood to avoid resource leaks

FastAPI sits on top of Starlette, which is a lightweight ASGI framework and toolkit. Every incoming HTTP request is handled by an async event loop (typically Uvicorn) which calls Starlette's router. FastAPI extends that router with parameter introspection: it reads each route function's signature using Python's

module and annotation metadata at startup, builds a Pydantic model for each route's inputs, and caches those models. At request time, parameters are extracted from the URL path, query string, headers, cookies, and body; Pydantic validates them; dependencies declared with
are resolved recursively; and the handler is called with fully typed, validated arguments. The return value is serialized through the
Pydantic model if one is declared, stripping undeclared fields before JSON encoding.
async context manager for initializing connection pools, loading model weights, or creating LLM client instances once at process start. The code before
runs on startup; the code after runs on shutdown. This cleanly pairs initialization with teardown in a single function.
Middleware is applied as ASGI wrappers around the entire application. Common middleware includes

for compression, and custom trace-id injection for distributed tracing. Middleware runs before route matching and applies universally. For per-route logic that needs path parameters or dependencies, route-level dependencies are the better tool.

The diagram below shows the full pipeline from HTTP request to response. Pydantic appears twice: once for input validation and once for output serialization via response_model. Dependency injection resolves between validation and the handler, giving each handler fully typed, ready-to-use values.

attrs.alt: FastAPI request/response pipeline: HTTP Request, Middleware, Route Matching, Pydantic Validation, Dependency Injection, Route Handler, Pydantic Serialization, HTTP Response

content.text: — exposing Python functions as HTTP tools that an agent can call, with automatic JSON schema generation from type hints
— endpoints that fan out multiple concurrent async calls to LLM providers and aggregate results
— retrieval-augmented generation pipelines that embed a query, search a vector DB, then call an LLM — all I/O-bound, all benefiting from async
— receiving model callbacks or third-party events that trigger downstream agent logic
— Model Context Protocol servers are plain HTTP servers; FastAPI's automatic schema generation aligns naturally with MCP's tool manifest approach

Install FastAPI and an ASGI server. Pydantic is a required dependency and installs automatically. The following example shows a complete basic app with Pydantic models, path parameters, query parameters, and lifespan management.

# pip install fastapi uvicorn[standard] httpx

from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from typing import Annotated

import httpx
from fastapi import FastAPI, Path, Query, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


# ── Pydantic models ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

class RunRequest(BaseModel):
    prompt: str = Field(..., min_length=1, max_length=4096)
    model: str = Field(default="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022")
    max_tokens: int = Field(default=1024, ge=1, le=8192)


class RunResponse(BaseModel):
    run_id: str
    status: str
    output: str | None = None


# ── Lifespan (startup / shutdown) ────────────────────────────────────────────

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    # Create a shared async HTTP client once per process
    app.state.http = httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=60.0)
    yield
    await app.state.http.aclose()


app = FastAPI(
    title="Agent API",
    version="1.0.0",
    lifespan=lifespan,
)


# ── Routes ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

@app.get("/health")
async def health() -> dict:
    return {"status": "ok"}


@app.post("/runs", response_model=RunResponse, status_code=201)
async def create_run(
    body: RunRequest,
    dry_run: Annotated[bool, Query(description="Skip the LLM call")] = False,
) -> RunResponse:
    """Start a new agent run and return its ID."""
    if dry_run:
        return RunResponse(run_id="dry-123", status="skipped")

    # body is already validated: prompt is str, max_tokens is int in [1, 8192]
    run_id = await _dispatch(body)
    return RunResponse(run_id=run_id, status="queued")


@app.get("/runs/{run_id}", response_model=RunResponse)
async def get_run(
    run_id: Annotated[str, Path(description="Run identifier")],
) -> RunResponse:
    record = await _fetch_run(run_id)
    if record is None:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail=f"Run {run_id!r} not found")
    return record


async def _dispatch(req: RunRequest) -> str:
    """Stub: replace with real agent dispatch logic."""
    return "run-abc"


async def _fetch_run(run_id: str) -> RunResponse | None:
    """Stub: replace with real database lookup."""
    return RunResponse(run_id=run_id, status="complete", output="Hello")
Dependency injection with a database session
is opening an async database session per request and guaranteeing it closes even on error. The dependency is declared once and reused across every route that needs it.

from collections.abc import AsyncGenerator
from typing import Annotated

from fastapi import Depends, FastAPI, HTTPException
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession, async_sessionmaker, create_async_engine
from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase, Mapped, mapped_column

DATABASE_URL = "sqlite+aiosqlite:///./agent.db"
engine = create_async_engine(DATABASE_URL, echo=False)
SessionLocal = async_sessionmaker(engine, expire_on_commit=False)


class Base(DeclarativeBase):
    pass


class AgentRun(Base):
    __tablename__ = "agent_runs"
    id: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(primary_key=True)
    prompt: Mapped[str]
    status: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(default="queued")


# ── Dependency ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

async def get_db() -> AsyncGenerator[AsyncSession, None]:
    """Yield an async DB session; always close it after the request."""
    async with SessionLocal() as session:
        yield session


# Annotated alias keeps route signatures readable
DBDep = Annotated[AsyncSession, Depends(get_db)]


# ── Routes ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

app = FastAPI()


@app.post("/runs", status_code=201)
async def create_run(prompt: str, db: DBDep) -> dict:
    run = AgentRun(prompt=prompt)
    db.add(run)
    await db.commit()
    await db.refresh(run)
    return {"id": run.id, "status": run.status}


@app.get("/runs/{run_id}")
async def get_run(run_id: int, db: DBDep) -> dict:
    run = await db.get(AgentRun, run_id)
    if run is None:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="not found")
    return {"id": run.id, "prompt": run.prompt, "status": run.status}
Background tasks run after the response is sent. For lightweight post-response side effects — sending a webhook notification, updating a cache —
is sufficient without a separate Celery cluster.

import asyncio
import httpx
from fastapi import BackgroundTasks, FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()


class EvalRequest(BaseModel):
    run_id: str
    output: str
    callback_url: str


async def _send_callback(url: str, payload: dict) -> None:
    """Fire a webhook after the response is returned to the caller."""
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=10) as client:
        try:
            await client.post(url, json=payload)
        except Exception:
            pass  # never let background errors surface to the caller


async def _run_eval(output: str) -> float:
    """Simulate an async LLM judge evaluation."""
    await asyncio.sleep(0)  # yield to event loop
    return 0.92


@app.post("/evals", status_code=202)
async def run_eval(
    body: EvalRequest,
    background_tasks: BackgroundTasks,
) -> dict:
    """Score an output immediately; fire a callback after the response."""
    score = await _run_eval(body.output)

    background_tasks.add_task(
        _send_callback,
        url=body.callback_url,
        payload={"run_id": body.run_id, "score": score},
    )

    # 202 returned now; callback fires after this response is sent
    return {"run_id": body.run_id, "score": score, "status": "callback_queued"}

A production RAG endpoint receives a user query, embeds it asynchronously, queries a vector store, and calls an LLM — three consecutive async I/O operations. With FastAPI this entire pipeline runs inside a single async handler, can be tested with

in-process, and the response shape is validated before the caller ever sees it. A team consuming the endpoint gets a live Swagger UI and can generate typed client code from the OpenAPI spec with no additional tooling work.

LangChain Serve, LlamaIndex's server module, and Haystack's API layer all use FastAPI as their HTTP transport. This is not accidental: validation and documentation come for free, and the async model matches how LLM calls are made. When you build a custom agentic backend, you are replicating the same design pattern those frameworks already chose.
Creating a new HTTP client per request
inside each handler skips connection pooling; create one client at startup on
— returning plain dicts means internal fields leak to clients and no output validation runs; always declare
Mutable default arguments in Pydantic models
— FastAPI returns 422 Unprocessable Entity on validation failure; client code that only handles 400/500 will silently swallow these
— split routes into APIRouter modules and compose them with
— the async context manager pattern pairs init with teardown; it is preferred over the deprecated
Declare Annotated type aliases for dependencies
declared once and reused across all routes that need it
— inputs often contain write-only fields (passwords, raw tokens) that must not appear in responses
Version your API with a prefix
makes non-breaking evolution straightforward and client contracts explicit
drives the ASGI app in-process; no running server needed
replaces real dependencies with test doubles without modifying production code

FastAPI's performance stems from two sources. First, Pydantic v2 uses a Rust core for validation, making schema checks orders of magnitude faster than pure-Python validators. Second, Uvicorn uses libuv under the hood, allowing the event loop to handle thousands of concurrent connections. For CPU-bound work — embedding generation, heavy data transformation — use

to prevent event loop starvation. Horizontal scaling is straightforward: FastAPI applications are stateless by design; run multiple Uvicorn workers with Gunicorn as the process manager, or deploy multiple container replicas behind a load balancer.
gives four processes, each handling many coroutines concurrently
— keep one DB connection pool and one HTTP client pool alive for the process lifetime; never create per-request
with an async generator to stream LLM tokens to the client as they arrive rather than buffering the full response
— standard ASGI server for development and production; install
— merges SQLAlchemy ORM models with Pydantic models, eliminating duplicate schemas for the DB layer and API layer
— database migration tool that pairs with SQLAlchemy; the standard choice for schema versioning in FastAPI + SQLAlchemy projects
drives the ASGI app in-process with no server
auto-instruments routes with spans, connecting API traces to downstream LLM call traces
reads environment variables into a validated Pydantic model, keeping config type-safe alongside the API schema
The table below compares the frameworks most commonly chosen for agentic Python backends across the dimensions that matter most for AI workloads.
as a first-class security dependency; token validation becomes a reusable dependency injected into any route that needs it
— FastAPI has no built-in rate limiter; use
or a reverse proxy for agent endpoints that call expensive LLM APIs
on string fields in Pydantic; unbounded inputs forwarded to an LLM can cause unexpected token charges
for custom exception types; avoid leaking internal stack traces to API consumers

Think of FastAPI as a compiler for your Python type hints. You write function signatures that declare what data each endpoint expects and produces. FastAPI reads those hints at import time and generates validation logic, serialization logic, and API documentation from them. The contract between caller and handler is expressed once in Python types — not duplicated in docstrings, not maintained separately in a YAML file. When the types are wrong at runtime, Pydantic catches it at the edge of your service, not deep inside your agent logic where the error is harder to trace.

The dependency injection system extends this philosophy to resources. Rather than reaching into global state for a database session or API key, each handler declares what it needs. FastAPI resolves the dependency graph, injects values, and cleans up after the request. The handler is a pure function of its typed inputs — testable in isolation by swapping dependencies, auditable by reading the function signature alone.
FastAPI turns Python type hints into validation, serialization, and OpenAPI documentation simultaneously — write the contract once and get all three.
Native ASGI async support is critical for agentic workloads that make many concurrent LLM and vector-DB calls; blocking calls inside async handlers stall the entire event loop.
The Depends() system is the correct place for shared resources — database sessions, authentication, API clients — not module-level globals.
Always declare response_model to prevent internal fields from leaking and to validate outgoing data, not just incoming data.
Create shared HTTP clients and DB pools at lifespan startup, not inside route handlers — connection reuse is critical for throughput under load.

FastAPI is the default choice for new Python AI/agent APIs; Flask remains valid for simple sync-only services; Django REST suits full-stack web apps; Starlette is for building frameworks, not applications.
Use app.dependency_overrides in tests to swap real dependencies with mocks — this makes FastAPI services highly testable without a running database or LLM.
